reason: Current unemployment level captures strong persistence in unemployment-rate forecasts.
Recent month-to-month unemployment change captures labor-market momentum.
Initial unemployment claims are a direct leading labor-market stress indicator and an LEI component.
Help-wanted to unemployed ratio proxies labor demand relative to available workers.
Payroll employment growth is a coincident labor-market condition and helps anchor unemployment movements.
Building permits are a cyclical leading indicator; weaker housing activity can precede weaker labor demand.
Stock prices summarize forward-looking expectations and are an LEI component.
Yield-curve slope is a business-cycle leading indicator; inversions often precede unemployment deterioration.
Consumer expectations are an LEI component and can anticipate spending/hiring weakness.
